description: First-principles reasoning toolkit to strip assumptions, decompose to fundamentals, and synthesize novel solutions under constraints.
ethical_guardrail: Use to improve truth-seeking and problem solving; do not use it to rationalize unethical outcomes or bypass safety, legal, or consent constraints.

rules.name: Assumption Inventory: Identify and Question All Assumptions
rules.principle: Hidden assumptions drive bad plans; make them explicit and testable before you build on them.
detection_triggers: We’re assuming a lot here
What if our assumptions are wrong?
This plan feels built on vibes
I don’t know what I’m taking for granted
How do I pressure-test the premise?
rules.insight_template: When {decision} feels shaky, apply assumption inventory: list assumptions about {customer}, {constraints}, {timeline}, and {resources}. For each, write a test {test} and what result would change your mind {falsifier}. Start by testing the riskiest assumption first.

rules.name: Irreducible Truths: Decompose to Fundamentals
rules.principle: Break the problem into facts that remain true even if opinions change; rebuild from those constraints.
detection_triggers: Let’s get to the fundamentals
What’s actually true here?
Everything feels complicated and fuzzy
I need a ground-truth model
How do I simplify this without losing reality?
rules.insight_template: When {problem} is messy, apply irreducible truths: list constraints that must hold (physics, math, legal, budget) as {truths}. Then rebuild a solution by combining those truths into a mechanism {mechanism} that could work even if other assumptions fail.

rules.name: Socratic Testing: Ask Better Questions Than Answers
rules.principle: Socratic questioning exposes contradictions and weak definitions by repeatedly asking for evidence, mechanisms, and alternatives.
detection_triggers: Why do we believe this?
What evidence supports that claim?
How exactly does that work?
What would prove this wrong?
What are we missing?
rules.insight_template: When {claim} is asserted, apply Socratic testing: ask (1) 'What do we mean by {term}?' (2) 'What evidence supports it?' (3) 'What mechanism links cause to effect?' (4) 'What would disconfirm it?' Then decide {next_step} based on answers, not confidence.

rules.name: Five Whys: Trace Symptoms to Root Cause
rules.principle: Repeatedly asking 'why' reveals causal chains and helps you target the true constraint instead of surface symptoms.
detection_triggers: We keep fixing the symptom
What’s the root cause here?
Why does this keep happening?
I need to get to the real issue
This problem is recurring
rules.insight_template: When {issue} repeats, apply Five Whys: ask 'Why?' five times until you hit a controllable cause {root_cause}. Then design {countermeasure} that changes the system so the cause can’t easily recur.

rules.name: Physics-as-Metaphor: Optimize by Constraints, Not Conventions
rules.principle: Treat problems like engineering: identify constraints, then design the simplest mechanism that satisfies them—even if it breaks tradition.
detection_triggers: That’s how it’s always been done
We’re copying competitors without thinking
What if we ignored industry norms?
How would an engineer solve this?
I want a clean, constraint-based approach
rules.insight_template: When {domain} is tradition-bound, apply physics-as-metaphor: list hard constraints {constraints} (time, energy, cost, safety) and optimize the mechanism {mechanism} to satisfy them. Ignore conventions unless they are justified by a constraint.

rules.name: Analogy vs First Principles: Use Analogy Carefully
rules.principle: Analogy transfers patterns, but first principles rebuilds truth; use analogy to generate hypotheses, not to prove them.
detection_triggers: It’s like Uber for X
We’re copying a playbook from another industry
This analogy feels persuasive but untested
Are we reasoning or just comparing?
I’m worried we’re using a bad analogy
rules.insight_template: When {analogy} drives the plan, apply the distinction: write what the analogy suggests {hypothesis}, then rebuild from first principles by listing the true constraints {constraints}. Keep the analogy only if it survives a falsification check {falsifier}.

rules.name: Cost Structure from Fundamentals: Build the Unit Economics
rules.principle: Pricing and viability come from fundamental cost drivers; estimate them bottom-up before believing projections.
detection_triggers: How much would this actually cost to deliver?
Our margins might be fake
We need to price this properly
Is this business model viable?
What are the real cost drivers?
rules.insight_template: When designing {offer}, apply fundamental cost reasoning: break delivery into components {components}, estimate time/cost per component, and compute unit economics {unit_econ}. Then set price {price} with margin of safety {buffer} and test willingness-to-pay with {validation_step}.

rules.name: Constraint First: Identify the Bottleneck Before Solutions
rules.principle: The real constraint governs throughput; solving non-constraints wastes effort and creates illusion of progress.
detection_triggers: We’re doing a lot but not moving
What’s the bottleneck here?
Everything feels urgent and tangled
We keep optimizing the wrong thing
Where should we focus first?
rules.insight_template: When {system} is stuck, apply constraint-first: find where work accumulates {queue_point} and name the constraint {constraint}. Solve only that with {constraint_action} until it moves, then re-scan. Ignore improvements that don’t increase end-to-end throughput.

rules.name: Inversion: Solve the Opposite Problem
rules.principle: By asking how to cause failure, you reveal hidden risks and necessary conditions for success.
detection_triggers: How do we avoid failure here?
What could go wrong?
I want to think like a pessimist for a minute
Let’s assume this fails—why?
How do I de-risk this plan?
rules.insight_template: When {plan} feels risky, apply inversion: list ways to guarantee failure {failure_modes}, then flip each into a safeguard {safeguards}. Add one margin-of-safety buffer {buffer} and define a kill-switch metric {kill_metric} that triggers stopping early.

rules.name: Synthesis: Rebuild Into a Novel Solution
rules.principle: After decomposition, recombine truths into a new mechanism and test it in the smallest viable experiment.
detection_triggers: Okay, how do we put this back together?
I have the pieces but not the solution
How do we turn analysis into action?
We need a novel approach
What’s the simplest experiment to test this?
rules.insight_template: When {analysis} is complete, apply synthesis: choose 2–3 core truths {truths}, design a mechanism {prototype} that satisfies them, then run a smallest test {experiment} with a clear success criterion {metric}. Iterate fast and retain wins into the new default.

CART-EPISTEMIC: Supplies falsification, base rates, and calibration so first-principles work stays grounded and testable.
CART-SYSTEMS-THINKING: Adds system maps, feedback loops, and leverage points so first-principles solutions target the right mechanism.
CART-UNIVERSAL-LAWS: Provides constraint, trade-off, inertia, and iteration principles that keep reasoning reality-aligned.
